content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 269

1. Symeon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ, to those who have obtained a faith of equal standing with ours through the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ:

2. May grace and peace be multiplied to you in the knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord.

3. As his divine power has granted to us all things pertaining to life and godliness, through the knowledge of him who called us by his own glory and virtue,

4. through which he has granted to us the precious and exceedingly great promises, so that through these you might become partakers of the divine nature, having escaped the corruption that is in the world through sinful desire.

5. And for this very reason, bringing all diligence, supply in your faith virtue, and in your virtue knowledge,

6. and in your knowledge self-control, and in your self-control endurance, and in your endurance godliness,

7. and in your godliness brotherly affection, and in your brotherly affection love.

8. For if these things are yours and are increasing, they render you neither idle nor unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.

9. For the one in whom these things are not present is blind, being shortsighted, having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of his former sins.

10. Therefore, brothers, be all the more diligent to make your calling and election firm; for if you do these things, you will never stumble.

11. For in this way the entrance into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ will be richly supplied to you.

12. Therefore I shall always be ready to remind you concerning these things, though you know them and have been established in the truth that is present with you.

13. And I consider it right, as long as I am in this tent, to stir you up by way of reminder,

14. knowing that the putting off of my tent is soon, even as our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me.

15. And I will also be diligent that at any time after my departure you may be able to call these things to remembrance.

16. For we did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.

17. For having received from God the Father honor and glory, when such a voice was borne to him by the Majestic Glory: "This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I am well pleased" --

18. and this voice we ourselves heard, borne from heaven, when we were with him on the holy mountain.

19. And we have the prophetic word made more sure, to which you do well to pay attention, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts.

20. Knowing this first of all, that no prophecy of Scripture comes about by one's own interpretation.

21. For no prophecy was ever brought forth by the will of man, but men spoke from God, being carried along by the Holy Spirit.

---

## Consensus Notes

1. **v.1 "Symeon":** All three agents preserve the Sinaiticus reading CYMECON (Symeon), the Hebraic form. PNEUMA rendered it "Simeon" (English phonetic equivalent); the consensus follows EMET and LOGOS in using "Symeon" to preserve the distinctive manuscript spelling.

2. **v.1 "servant" vs. "slave":** EMET renders doulos as "slave" (literal), while LOGOS uses "servant" and PNEUMA uses "servant." The consensus adopts "servant" as the majority reading that conveys the relational sense while acknowledging the term implies complete devotion.

3. **v.1 Granville Sharp construction:** All three agents correctly identify "our God and Savior Jesus Christ" as a single-referent construction. No disagreement on theological import.

4. **v.4 "exceedingly great":** EMET reads "greatest" (megista, superlative), LOGOS reads "exceedingly great," PNEUMA reads "great." The consensus follows LOGOS as a balanced rendering of the superlative megista.

5. **v.9 "having received forgetfulness":** EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hellenistic idiom lethen labon. PNEUMA simplifies to "forgetting." The consensus retains the more formal rendering to preserve the deliberate quality implied by the Greek.

6. **v.13 "tent" vs. "body":** EMET and LOGOS retain the literal "tent" (skenoma); PNEUMA renders dynamically as "body." The consensus retains "tent" as the biblical metaphor is well understood and theologically significant.

7. **v.17 Divine voice wording:** Minor variation exists among agents. The consensus follows the Sinaiticus reading closely: "This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I am well pleased." PNEUMA paraphrased as "dearly loved Son, who brings me great joy," which departs from the manuscript.

8. **v.19 "made more sure" vs. "greater confidence":** EMET and LOGOS render bebaioteronas "more firm/more sure" (comparative adjective modifying "prophetic word"). PNEUMA reframes as "even greater confidence in the message." The consensus preserves the adjectival construction modifying the prophetic word itself.

9. **v.20-21 Structure:** EMET begins v.20 with "This first knowing"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Knowing this first of all" / "Above all, you must realize." The consensus follows the LOGOS phrasing as formally accurate and readable.

-- SOPHIA, Consensus Arbiter
